title: Hot Cocoa Gift Crate
summary: A little slatted crate to fill with hot cocoa supplies as a gift. Two 1x6 ends, a bottom, and four lath slats — under an hour.
description:
A small open crate sized to hold hot-cocoa fixings — mugs, marshmallows, packets, a bar of chocolate — as a holiday gift. It's built from scraps: two 1x6 end pieces, a 1x6 bottom, and four thin lath strips for the sides, with a stained finish and a chalkboard label on the front.

There's essentially no joinery. Glue and brad-nail the ends to the bottom, then run the lath strips across to form the open sides. Because the slats are thin, use 1-1/4" brads or staples and don't overdrive them.

Stain rather than paint suits the rustic gift look, and a chalkboard label — bought, or made with chalkboard paint and a stencil or cutting machine — lets you write the recipient's name or 'Hot Cocoa' on the front. Fill it, add a bow, and it's a gift that outlasts what's inside it.

tools.note: Crosscuts; a hand saw works for this little.

materials.name: Chalkboard label
materials.note: Or make your own with chalkboard paint.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut two ends at 4" and one bottom at 16" from the 1x6, and four side slats at 17-1/2" from the lath. Those three 1x6 pieces total exactly 24", which is the whole 2 ft board with nothing left over for the saw kerf — start from a longer offcut or buy a little extra, or the second end will finish a shade under 4". The four 17-1/2" slats use 70" of the 6 ft of lath, which leaves plenty of margin. Square every cut: on a crate this small an out-of-square end shows immediately, and there is no joinery here to hide it.

steps.title: Attach the ends to the bottom
steps.body: Stand the two 4" end pieces on edge at each end of the 16" bottom and butt them against the bottom's end grain, so each end runs the full height and the bottom fits between them. That makes the crate 17-1/2" long overall — 16" plus 3/4" of end at each end — which is exactly what the 17-1/2" slats are cut to, and it makes the finished crate 4" tall with 3-1/4" of usable depth above the 3/4" bottom. Do not sit the ends on top of the bottom instead: that reading gives a 16" crate and every slat will overhang by 3/4" at each end. Glue both joints and drive three or four 1-1/4" brads through each end into the bottom, holding the end square to the bottom as you nail since end grain gives a brad very little to grip.

steps.title: Add the side slats
steps.body: Run two 17-1/2" lath strips along each side, glued and nailed to the outside faces of both end pieces so their ends finish flush with the ends of the crate. Two 1-1/2" strips cover 3" of the 3-1/4" of side available above the bottom, so set the lower strip down against the bottom board and the upper flush with the top of the ends, and let the small gap fall in the middle — that gap is what gives the crate its slatted look. The lath is only 1/4" thick and splits at the drop of a hat, so keep the brads back from the ends, dial the nailer pressure down, and use staples if you have them. Check the crate sits flat on the bench before the glue sets.

steps.title: Stain and add the label
steps.body: Stain the crate, then attach the chalkboard label to the front with glue and write on it. Fill with hot-cocoa supplies and gift it.

images.alt: Small slatted wooden gift crate filled with hot cocoa supplies
